dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 5 for traumatic injury. Located at a basketball court.
Hi, I'm Sam, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
He fell and hit his head. Was breathing fast and confused initially.
Is he conscious now?
Yes, he has been answering us since arriving, but was unclear at first.
Is he following commands or responding to questions at the moment?
Yes, following simple commands now, but initially just looked confused.
Does he open his eyes when prompted?
Yes, he did.
How clear are his verbal responses currently?
Clearer now, but earlier he said he couldn't remember the fall.
Does he move both arms and legs on command?
Yes, but movements look weak, said he has no strength in his limbs.
He is conscious, breathing, and no signs of airway obstruction detected. C-spine precaution in place.

dialogue.micro_intent: check airway - Preferred posture to maintain airway
dialogue.utterance: Manual C-spine maintained by R-FD. I will use a two-hand hold as well for additional support.

dialogue.micro_intent: check breathing - Rate and depth of respirations
dialogue.utterance: Respiratory rate is 32, breaths are shallow and slightly labored, but not grunting or retracting.
He is in moderate respiratory distress; pursed lips, but no obvious use of accessory muscles at this time.

dialogue.micro_intent: check breathing - Oxygen saturation measured with pulse oximetry
dialogue.utterance: Oxygen saturation measured at 100% on room air upon initial assessment.

dialogue.micro_intent: check circulation - Pulse rate and quality
dialogue.utterance: Pulse is 111, which is tachycardic, but strong and regular in rhythm and amplitude on both radial pulses.
Skin is pale but no signs of cyanosis or pallor. No mottling observed.
Blood pressure is 146/90 mmHg on initial check. No evidence of hypotension or hypertension at the moment.

dialogue.micro_intent: S.A.M.P.L.E. - events leading up to the event
dialogue.utterance: Can you explain what led to the fall, patient?

dialogue.micro_intent: S.A.M.P.L.E. - events leading up to the event
dialogue.utterance: I was playing basketball, jumped for the ball, then suddenly hit my head. Everything went black or something like that.
What symptoms are you having right now?
Neck pain, confusion, no memory of the event or right after. I also feel like I'm having trouble breathing sometimes.
On a scale from 1 to 10, how would you rate the pain in your neck at this time?
It's at about an 8 right now. Feels bad.
I will do a finger stick to check your blood glucose now, as part of routine checks. Let me prepare for that.
Applying 3-lead ECG for monitoring. We weren't able to do 4-lead at the moment due to field priorities, but this will help us monitor and recheck once stabilized.
Blood glucose reading remains at 0, but we noted there is no hypoglycemic symptoms. Possibly pre-existing condition, Suboxone patient mentioned later on.
EKG tracing shows sinus tachycardia at the moment. Minimal ST changes, but expected with distress or pain mechanisms-it deserves further transport monitoring.
You will now be receiving a full spinal immobilization with a cervical collar and backboard for safe transport. This reduces risk of further injury until we reach the hospital.

We'll be applying a rigid cervical collar now. You may feel pressure, but it's to keep you safe during this process. Next, we will lower you onto the backboard for full body immobilization with straps and padding.
Spinal restriction is now complete. Full immobilization in place with collar, padding, and backboard. You are stable for movement now.

dialogue.micro_intent: head and neck exam - Mental Status - A&O
dialogue.utterance: Your level of awareness has improved, from only knowing yourself (A&O 1) to being A&O 4. You are now oriented to more than yourself, responding appropriately to time, place, and people with some effort.

dialogue.micro_intent: head and neck exam - Nuchal rigidity
dialogue.utterance: You do express pain in the neck but show mobility albeit with limpness and weakness. No signs of nuchal rigidity or occipital pressure pain noted so far during manual inspection.
I've performed a gross neurologic check and limb examination-no deformities or angulations detected in spine or extremities during assessment on backboard integration.
Per the ePCR field impression, we will activate the Head Injury Protocol 4-6 as we suspect possible traumatic injury to the head and neck area given the event history and your GCS of 14.
Taking another pulse check now-pulse is now 93 - still tachycardic, but somewhat improved from earlier. It is maintaining a strong volume in both arms.
Respiratory rate is 27 now, and depth has improved slightly after coaching-but shallow and at times labored. No retraction noted visually.
Blood pressure is 145/90 now-stable, no drastic drops or spikes recorded so far.
SaO2 is now lower at 88%-this could be due to the increased respiratory rate or patient distress. We are watching for further decline.

IV lock has been placed with 18-gauge peripheral IV at the right anterior cubital vein as noted in ePCR, and immobilization has been secured with collar and board based on field observation and protocol.
On a 1 to 10 scale, where does your pain rank now compared to the initial report of 8?
Still around 8. Still feeling the neck pain, and it's a bit harder to breathe now that I am immobilized, but manageable.
Any changes in symptoms during the last小时内?
Some breathlessness, but nothing else really changes. Head feels heavy and thoughts are still jumbled. I can't recall anything before the fall or after.
Which facility would you prefer for your transport, patient? Do you have a personal preference?.
I can go anywhere-VCU Medical Center is fine to me since that is where I've been in the past when I've had injuries.
Taking this time to review and confirm immobilization and patient restraint measures-no compromises observed at this point prior to movement.
We're preparing to transfer you to the ambulance now. Please hold on as we assist you onto the stretcher, and stay still as much as possible for your safety.
Transfer to the ambulance is progressing. Straps and stabilizers are kept intact and patient is kept on a 180-degree backboard, per best practices outlined in ePCR.
No cyanosis or pallor noted consistent with field vitals. Skin is still pink and unmarred, with no signs of trauma-related discoloration on extremities or torso at this point per current view.

dialogue.micro_intent: head and neck exam - Pupillary reflex
dialogue.utterance: Bilateral pupils are equal in size, round, and reactive to light from both sides-no anisocoria or fixed pupils observed using penlight during secondary survey.

dialogue.role: nose, mouth and throat exam - Drainage; Medic
dialogue.utterance: No visible drainage from ears or nose consistent with ePCR report. No hemorrhage, ecchymosis, or eye trauma observed to date.
Bilateral breath sounds are clear and equal, no wheezing, crackles, or rales with lung auscultation using field equipment. No asymmetry in breathing observed from either side.
Abdomen is flat and soft, no guarding, tenderness, or distension observed. Bowel sounds were not prominent due to immobilization, but ePCR indicated no abdominal trauma.

dialogue.micro_intent: extremities and back exam - Gross motor and sensory function
dialogue.utterance: Upper and lower extremities show no deformity. However, as mentioned in field notes, both sides show moderate weakness-equal in both arms and legs-but no localized sensory loss or numbness observed per neurological check.
No abnormal rashes, scars, or lesions noted during secondary body check-skin condition remains consistent across entire surface area observed.
Vehicle is fully prepared and transport-ready. All systems have been reviewed and confirmed stable-no compromises to immobilization or IV access during the transfer process.
You are now fully immobilized and safe for transport to VCU Medical Center. We will monitor you constantly throughout the ambulance ride for any changes in status or signs of deterioration.
Retaking pulse now. Rate is now 93-still slightly tachycardic, but stronger and more stable rhythm observed from previous to this point.
Respiratory rate again assessed at 27-shallow, but with minimal signs of respiratory distress now. SaO2 is monitored to reflect current oxygen levels during movement.
Oxygen saturation remains at 88%. We are keeping a close eye on this and ensuring you have an unobstructed airway during transport.
Initial vitals and interventions stable-SaO2 is 88 at this moment, IV line and immobilization in place. Spinal precautions remain in effect as described in ePCR as part of trauma assessment.
Main medical focus will be head trauma and neurological status, with reported pain at level 8 as observed. Patient is currently alert and oriented at A&O 4 with minimal verbal disorientation.
Vitals just done-ECG shows normal sinus rhythm without evidence of acute tachycardia or arrhythmia from last check. No ST elevation or ischemic changes per ongoing monitoring.
Patient ready for ambulance boarding now-no delays noted in securing all equipment and preparing for immobilization and transport without assistance needed beyond basic transfer.
Completed placement of a four-lead ECG monitor in compliance with ePCR for ongoing ischemia and dysrhythmia monitoring during transport. Patient has shown no further complications.
No medications administered per ePCR notes. Blood glucose check done, reading at 0-no hypoglycemic maneuvers were performed per patient's condition or protocol.
Full immobilization is in place using cervical collar and backboard. Under protocol 4-6 for suspected head injury, all spine precautions are being taken as written in field impressions.
The ambulance has been loaded and all safety systems confirmed. Patient secured for movement with full spinal stabilization in place as per ePCR requirements for transport.
When did your symptoms first begin?
About 30 to 45 minutes ago before I called 911. I was utterly unaware of the fall at first.
Transport destination is now confirmed as VCU Medical Center for definitive care in light of head impact and reported confusion in injury mechanism.
Patient's neurological status has improved from A&O 1 to A&O 4. No further decline in cognition noted at point of handoff, and all life signs are stable.
Blood glucose has been rechecked in recent steps, and remains at baseline 0 reading-no new medications or fluids have altered the status.
The Glasgow Coma Scale assessment shows GCS 14 (E4, V4, M6) per ePCR logs-as previously stated, no further changes noted at time of boarding.
I don't have any known drug allergies, and Suboxone is the only medication I regularly use at this time.
Emergency Medical Services Protocol 4-6 for suspected head injury has been completed as per field impression and ongoing status in ePCR.
Patient lying flat and secured on backboard with no compromise in immobilization or oxygenation status-no visible changes during final phase of loading and transport setup.
EKG is in active monitoring mode-continuing as appropriate during ambulance transit and expected at VCU ER. All field-generated logs and paperwork are now in order for handoff.
No medications administered during patient care and transport. Based on current vitals and field impression, there are no current indications for medication administration at this time.

dialogue.micro_intent: redo HPI - pertinent past medical history
dialogue.utterance: Are there other chronic conditions or drug use I should be aware of for the hospital as part of your history?

dialogue.micro_intent: redo HPI - pertinent past medical history
dialogue.utterance: Nothing significant except that I use Suboxone regularly-even mentioned that in field interview prior to your arrival, if that helps.
All equipment is now verified and tracked as active during patient transport. No further interventions initiated at this time.
Patient is mentally alert for current time, with GCS 14, oriented to person, place, and event, though time awareness and clarity remain incomplete per rechecking.
All systems reviewed and confirmed ready for transport. Patient is currently stable with no notable changes-with GCS and protocol status as noted.
Patient ready for handover, with no ongoing signs of distress beyond what is already logged.
